case_name: National Insurance Co. Ltd. Vs. Harpreet Singh and others.
court: High Court of Jammu and Kashmir at Jammu

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: S. Amarjeet Singh (deceased) - claimants Harbaksh Singh & Harpreet Singh
compensation_breakdown.notes: Tribunal awarded Rs.17,53,800 (Loss of dependency Rs.17,38,800 + funeral Rs.15,000); High Court reduced total to Rs.12,51,600 (Loss of dependency Rs.12,36,600 + funeral Rs.15,000).

facts: On 29.09.2007 S. Amarjeet Singh was travelling by car (Reg. No. JK02D-9212) from Kishtwar to Jammu when the car driven by owner Inderjit Singh Bali plunged into a trench near Thathri. The deceased suffered grievous injuries and died. His two sons (respondents/claimants) filed a claim under section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1998 against the owner and the insurer National Insurance Co. Ltd. The Motor Accident Claims Tribunal awarded Rs.17,53,800 which was appealed by the insurer.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued that under section 166 all legal representatives of the deceased are entitled to claim compensation irrespective of dependency and relied on judgments such as Smt. Lalitha and Kulsum Bai to support entitlement. They supported the Tribunal's calculation of income and family strength used to compute loss of dependency.
arguments_respondent: The insurer/appellant contended that only dependents are entitled to compensation under 'loss of dependency' and that respondent Harpreet Singh was gainfully employed and not dependent on the deceased. The insurer also challenged the computation of the deceased's effective income and the Tribunal's deduction towards personal expenses.
ratio_decidendi: "award of compensation under the head, 'loss of dependency' is governed by the number of dependents of the deceased and not the number of legal representatives having filed the claim." The court held that "Only such legal representatives of the deceased who at the time of the death were dependent upon him are relevant for calculating compensation payable under the head 'loss of dependency'." It further stated that "a legal representative, who was not dependent upon the deceased, is not entitled to compensation under the head 'loss of dependency'", but such a claimant may still claim "compensation for loss to estate as well as the statutory compensation under section 140 of the Act" and that "the quantum cannot be less than the liability referable to Section 140 of the Act." Applying these principles, the court found Harpreet Singh was not dependent, reduced the family strength for dependency to two, recalculated the multiplicand and multiplier and thus reduced the compensation.
final_order: Appeal allowed in part. Impugned judgment modified: Loss of dependency reduced to Rs.12,36,600; Funeral expenses Rs.15,000; Total Rs.12,51,600. Rate of interest and other conditions of the impugned award to apply; record remitted to the Tribunal.
summary: This is an appeal by National Insurance Co. Ltd against a Tribunal award in a motor-accident death (29.09.2007) of S. Amarjeet Singh. The High Court applied Sarla Verma and related precedents to hold that only those legal representatives who were dependents at the time of death are entitled to 'loss of dependency'. Because one son (Harpreet) was gainfully employed and not dependent, the court reduced the family strength and recalculated compensation, decreasing the total award from Rs.17,53,800 to Rs.12,51,600. The insurer's appeal was allowed in part and the award was modified accordingly.
embedding_summary: National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Harpreet Singh & Ors., High Court of Jammu & Kashmir, CIMA No. D-627/2010; CMA No. 933/2010, judgment dated 03.02.2014. Accident on 29.09.2007 near Thathri involving car Reg. JK02D-9212 (owner/driver Inderjit Singh Bali) causing death of S. Amarjeet Singh. Claim under Section 166 MV Act, 1998 by two sons; Tribunal awarded Rs.17,53,800. Key legal issues: entitlement to 'loss of dependency' (whether all legal representatives or only dependents), application of multiplier method, deductions for personal expenses, and interplay with Section 140 statutory compensation. Court applied Sarla Verma, Kerala State RTC v. Susamma Thomas, Trilok Chandra and Manjuri Bera, held "award of compensation under the head, 'loss of dependency' is governed by the number of dependents" and non-dependent legal representative not entitled to loss of dependency but entitled to at least Section 140 relief. Final modified award: Loss of dependency Rs.12,36,600; Funeral Rs.15,000; Total Rs.12,51,600; interest and other terms of original award to apply.
legal_principles: Loss of dependency is governed by the actual number of dependents, not the number of legal representatives who file claims
A legal representative who was not dependent at the time of death is not entitled to compensation under 'loss of dependency'
A non-dependent legal representative can still claim statutory compensation (Section 140) and loss to the estate; "the quantum cannot be less than the liability referable to Section 140 of the Act"
Deduction towards personal living expenses must follow Sarla Verma standardized deductions (1/3 for 2-3 dependents, 1/4 for 4-6, 1/5 for >6)
Future prospects addition depends on age (no addition where deceased was over 50) and multiplier selection is per Sarla Verma's table
Tribunal's factual findings on income are to be respected unless contrary evidence shows error
cases_cited: Sarla Verma and ors. v. Delhi Transport Corporation, 2009 ACJ 1298
Kerala State Road Transport Corporation v. Susamma Thomas, (1994) II SCC 176
U.P. State Road Transport Corporation v. Trilok Chandra, (1996) 4 SCC 362
Manjuri Bera v. Oriental Company Limited and Anr., 2007 (3) Supreme 620
Smt. Lalitha and ors. v. Dashanbhat Haribansh Bhat, AIR 1998 Karnataka 344
Kulsum Bai v. Kallu and ors., AIR 200 MP 292
bm25_text: National Insurance Co. Ltd. Vs. Harpreet Singh and others. High Court of Jammu and Kashmir at Jammu 2014 On 29.09.2007 S. Amarjeet Singh was travelling by car (Reg. No. JK02D-9212) from Kishtwar to Jammu when the car driven by owner Inderjit Singh Bali plunged into a trench near Thathri. The deceased suffered grievous injuries and died. His two sons (respondents/claimants) filed a claim under section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1998 against the owner and the insurer National Insurance Co. Ltd. The Motor Accident Claims Tribunal awarded Rs.17,53,800 which was appealed by the insurer. Claimants argued that under section 166 all legal representatives of the deceased are entitled to claim compensation irrespective of dependency and relied on judgments such as Smt. Lalitha and Kulsum Bai to support entitlement. They supported the Tribunal's calculation of income and family strength used to compute loss of dependency. The insurer/appellant contended that only dependents are entitled to compensation under 'loss of dependency' and that respondent Harpreet Singh was gainfully employed and not dependent on the deceased. The insurer also challenged the computation of the deceased's effective income and the Tribunal's deduction towards personal expenses. "award of compensation under the head, 'loss of dependency' is governed by the number of dependents of the deceased and not the number of legal representatives having filed the claim." The court held that "Only such legal representatives of the deceased who at the time of the death were dependent upon him are relevant for calculating compensation payable under the head 'loss of dependency'." It further stated that "a legal representative, who was not dependent upon the deceased, is not entitled to compensation under the head 'loss of dependency'", but such a claimant may still claim "compensation for loss to estate as well as the statutory compensation under section 140 of the Act" and that "the quantum cannot be less than the liability referable to Section 140 of the Act." Applying these principles, the court found Harpreet Singh was not dependent, reduced the family strength for dependency to two, recalculated the multiplicand and multiplier and thus reduced the compensation. Appeal allowed in part. Impugned judgment modified: Loss of dependency reduced to Rs.12,36,600; Funeral expenses Rs.15,000; Total Rs.12,51,600. Rate of interest and other conditions of the impugned award to apply; record remitted to the Tribunal. This is an appeal by National Insurance Co. Ltd against a Tribunal award in a motor-accident death (29.09.2007) of S. Amarjeet Singh. The High Court applied Sarla Verma and related precedents to hold that only those legal representatives who were dependents at the time of death are entitled to 'loss of dependency'. Because one son (Harpreet) was gainfully employed and not dependent, the court reduced the family strength and recalculated compensation, decreasing the total award from Rs.17,53,800 to Rs.12,51,600. The insurer's appeal was allowed in part and the award was modified accordingly. National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Key legal issues: entitlement to 'loss of dependency' (whether all legal representatives or only dependents), application of multiplier method, deductions for personal expenses, and interplay with Section 140 statutory compensation. Court applied Sarla Verma, Kerala State RTC v. Susamma Thomas, Trilok Chandra and Manjuri Bera, held "award of compensation under the head, 'loss of dependency' is governed by the number of dependents" and non-dependent legal representative not entitled to loss of dependency but entitled to at least Section 140 relief. Final modified award: Loss of dependency Rs.12,36,600; Funeral Rs.15,000; Total Rs.12,51,600; interest and other terms of original award to apply. S.
